questions.passage:
Political arguments frequently become arguments about definitions. One side insists that a contested policy counts as a right; another calls it a benefit. A movement names a condition exploitation; its opponents describe the same arrangement as exchange. Observers then divide into two camps. Some announce that whoever controls the definition has already won. Others dismiss the dispute as “mere semantics” and demand a return to substantive facts. Both responses misunderstand what definitions do in public life.

A definition does not merely attach a label to a settled object. It selects features as relevant, groups cases that might otherwise appear separate, and establishes what must be shown next. Calling something a right does not by itself prove that institutions should protect it, but it changes the burden of argument. The opponent may now be asked to justify interference rather than the claimant being asked to justify provision. Definitions organize the sequence of reasons. They decide which claims appear exceptional and which arrive already dressed as presumptions.

Yet they cannot complete that sequence. A person who defines freedom as noninterference has not established that noninterference should outweigh equality, security, or material capacity. A rival who defines freedom as effective power has not proved which resources must be supplied or by whom. The definitions expose different priorities, but the conflict among those priorities remains. Verbal victory cannot substitute for institutional judgment.

This is why dictionary appeals are usually disappointing. Dictionaries record conventional usage across contexts that political disputes are attempting to reorganize. They can reveal whether a speaker is using a term idiosyncratically, but they cannot decide whether an inherited usage should govern a new circumstance. Nor can historical origin settle the matter. A word’s earliest meaning may illuminate a forgotten distinction while possessing no automatic authority over present institutions. Genealogy can unsettle certainty, but it cannot legislate by itself or relieve present institutions of judgment.

The proper analysis of a political definition therefore has three parts. First, ask what cases it includes and excludes. Second, identify the burden of justification it assigns. Third, trace the institutional consequences that would follow if the definition were adopted. A broad definition may express admirable concern while creating duties no institution can discharge. A narrow definition may preserve administrative clarity while rendering a genuine injury officially invisible. Neither defect can be discovered by examining wording alone. Institutional imagination must accompany verbal analysis, or elegant boundaries will conceal practical disorder.

Definitions also have a temporal dimension. Once embedded in law or administration, they generate records, offices, and expectations. A category created for one purpose may later become the basis for funding, representation, or eligibility. Altering it then affects people who have arranged their conduct around its prior boundaries. The dispute is no longer simply about accurate description; it concerns the cost of revising an institutional world built from that description.

Political definitions are therefore neither sovereign commands nor decorative labels. They are proposals for organizing attention and responsibility. They deserve scrutiny because they can make claims visible, shift burdens, and shape institutions. They deserve resistance when advocates pretend that naming has already supplied the values and judgments still required. The most revealing reply to a proposed definition is not “What does the word really mean?” but “What work will this boundary perform, and who will be required to live with it?”
questions.stem: Which statement best captures the passage's main argument?

choices.A: Political definitions structure responsibility without settling the values at stake.
choices.B: Dictionary usage should govern public disputes unless institutions adopt new terms.
choices.C: Definitions matter only after they have been incorporated into formal law.
choices.D: Political conflict can be resolved by choosing the most inclusive available category.

explanations.A: Correct. The author argues that definitions select cases, shift burdens, and shape institutions while leaving substantive value conflicts unresolved.
explanations.B: Dictionaries can reveal unusual usage but cannot determine whether inherited language should control new circumstances.
explanations.C: Definitions organize reasons before legal adoption and gain additional effects once embedded in institutions.
explanations.D: Broad categories can create impossible duties, so inclusiveness alone does not resolve the dispute.

questions.stem: The contrast between defining freedom as noninterference and defining it as effective power primarily demonstrates that

choices.A: political terms should retain their oldest established meanings.
choices.B: rival definitions usually conceal identical policy commitments.
choices.C: definitions reveal priorities without ranking competing values.
choices.D: freedom cannot be defined clearly enough for institutional use.

explanations.A: Historical origin is later denied automatic authority over present arrangements.
explanations.B: The two definitions direct attention toward different priorities and possible duties.
explanations.C: Correct. Each definition frames the issue, yet neither establishes how freedom should be balanced against other values or translated into policy.
explanations.D: The passage does not abandon definition; it proposes a disciplined method for evaluating definitions.

questions.stem: Which claim is most strongly implied by the passage?

choices.A: A category becomes more accurate when institutions use it consistently over time.
choices.B: Administrative clarity is usually more important than recognition of injury.
choices.C: Changing a definition affects only future applicants or claimants.
choices.D: Reliance can make category revision costly even when justified.

explanations.A: Repeated use creates expectations but does not guarantee descriptive or moral accuracy.
explanations.B: The author presents clarity and visibility as competing concerns without establishing a general hierarchy.
explanations.C: People may already have arranged conduct around prior boundaries, so present stakeholders can be affected.
explanations.D: Correct. Embedded categories generate offices, records, eligibility, and expectations that complicate later change without making change automatically wrong.

questions.stem: A city debates whether app-based drivers should be classified as employees. According to the passage, the most useful next step would be to examine

choices.A: which dictionary lists the older meaning of employee.
choices.B: which duties and protections the classification would trigger.
choices.C: whether either side can claim exclusive ownership of the term.
choices.D: whether the broadest possible category receives public support.

explanations.A: Dictionary history cannot decide whether inherited usage should govern a new labor arrangement.
explanations.B: Correct. The author’s method asks what the boundary includes, how it assigns justification, and what institutional consequences follow.
explanations.C: The passage rejects the idea that control of a term itself completes the political argument.
explanations.D: Public support for breadth does not reveal whether resulting duties are coherent or sustainable.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the passage's account of institutional consequences?

choices.A: A revised eligibility definition shifts funding and settled expectations.
choices.B: Citizens disagree about the emotional force of several political labels.
choices.C: Older dictionaries list multiple meanings for the same political term.
choices.D: Speakers often choose shorter definitions during public debate.

explanations.A: Correct. Changes in funding and settled expectations would show that definitions build institutional worlds whose revision affects more than verbal description.
explanations.B: Emotional disagreement confirms that labels matter but does not isolate the institutional effects emphasized in the later paragraphs.
explanations.C: Multiple recorded meanings support skepticism toward simple dictionary appeals but not the claim about embedded categories.
explanations.D: Definition length has no clear bearing on burdens, boundaries, or institutional reliance.

questions.stem: The author would most likely agree that a political definition should be evaluated by

choices.A: whether it eliminates disagreement among opposing groups.
choices.B: whether it matches the earliest documented use of the term.
choices.C: the boundaries, burdens, and institutional effects it creates.
choices.D: the confidence with which its advocates present it.

explanations.A: Definitions can clarify the structure of disagreement without eliminating underlying conflicts of value.
explanations.B: Historical origin may illuminate a distinction but receives no automatic governing authority.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage explicitly proposes assessing inclusion, assigned justification, and practical institutional consequences.
explanations.D: Advocates may mistake naming for completed argument, so confidence is not a reliable standard.
